questions.questionText: The LH surge triggers:
questions.optionB: Ovulation (approximately 36 hours after the LH surge)
questions.explanation: LH surge: triggered by rising estrogen from the dominant follicle (positive feedback effect when estrogen > 200 pg/mL for >48 hours). Ovulation occurs ~36 hours after the LH surge (~24 hours after the LH peak), typically on day 14 of a 28-day cycle. The LH surge also triggers: completion of meiosis I in the oocyte, luteinization of granulosa cells, and prostaglandin/protease release for follicular rupture. Reference: Guyton — Ovulation.

questions.questionText: The dominant hormone of the luteal phase is:
questions.optionB: Progesterone (from the corpus luteum)
questions.explanation: After ovulation, the ruptured follicle becomes the corpus luteum, which produces progesterone (predominant) and estrogen. Progesterone: converts proliferative endometrium to secretory (prepares for implantation), raises basal body temperature (~0.5°C — used for ovulation detection), thickens cervical mucus, maintains early pregnancy. If no pregnancy → corpus luteum degenerates after ~14 days → progesterone drops → menstruation. Reference: Guyton — Luteal Phase.

questions.questionText: hCG (human chorionic gonadotropin) is produced by:
questions.optionB: Syncytiotrophoblast of the placenta
questions.explanation: hCG is produced by the syncytiotrophoblast soon after implantation (~6-8 days). Functions: maintains corpus luteum (prevents luteolysis) → continues progesterone production during first trimester until placenta takes over at ~10-12 weeks. hCG peaks at 10-12 weeks then declines. Structurally similar to LH (shares α subunit). Basis of pregnancy tests. β-hCG is specific. Elevated in: molar pregnancy, choriocarcinoma. Reference: Guyton — hCG.

questions.questionText: Testosterone is primarily produced by:
questions.optionB: Leydig cells (interstitial cells of testes)
questions.explanation: Leydig cells (interstitial cells between seminiferous tubules): produce testosterone in response to LH. Testosterone functions: spermatogenesis (via Sertoli cells), male secondary sexual characteristics, anabolic effects (muscle/bone), deepening of voice, libido. Sertoli cells: support spermatogenesis, produce inhibin B (negative feedback on FSH), secrete ABP (androgen-binding protein), form blood-testis barrier. Governed by HPG axis. Reference: Guyton — Male Reproduction.

questions.questionText: Inhibin B from Sertoli cells selectively inhibits:
questions.optionB: FSH secretion from the anterior pituitary
questions.explanation: Inhibin B is produced by Sertoli cells (in males) and granulosa cells (in females). It specifically inhibits FSH secretion from the anterior pituitary (negative feedback). This provides selective regulation of FSH without affecting LH. Activin has the opposite effect (stimulates FSH). In males with Sertoli cell damage → low inhibin B → elevated FSH (FSH used as marker of spermatogenic function). Testosterone provides negative feedback on both LH and FSH. Reference: Guyton — Inhibin.

questions.questionText: The most fertile period (ovulation) in a regular 28-day cycle occurs on approximately day:
questions.optionB: Day 14
questions.explanation: In a regular 28-day cycle, ovulation occurs on day 14 (14 days before the next menstrual period). The luteal phase is relatively constant (~14 days). The follicular phase varies. Fertile window: ~day 10-17 (sperm survive 3-5 days in female tract, ovum viable for 24 hours after ovulation). For irregular cycles: ovulation = total cycle length - 14 days. Ovulation detection: LH surge in urine, basal body temperature rise, cervical mucus changes. Reference: Guyton — Menstrual Cycle.

questions.questionText: GnRH is released from the hypothalamus in a:
questions.optionB: Pulsatile fashion (essential for normal LH/FSH secretion)
questions.explanation: GnRH is released in pulses every 60-90 minutes. Pulsatile release is ESSENTIAL for normal gonadotropin secretion. Continuous GnRH (or long-acting GnRH agonists like leuprolide) paradoxically SUPPRESSES LH/FSH after an initial stimulation (downregulation of GnRH receptors). This is used therapeutically: prostate cancer, endometriosis, precocious puberty, IVF protocols. GnRH antagonists (cetrorelix) cause immediate suppression. Reference: Guyton — GnRH Pulsatility.

questions.questionText: During pregnancy, the placenta produces all of the following hormones EXCEPT:
questions.optionB: Human placental lactogen (hPL)
questions.explanation: Placental hormones: hCG (maintains corpus luteum early), hPL/hCS (anti-insulin/diabetogenic — ensures glucose supply to fetus, promotes breast development), progesterone (after 10-12 weeks, placenta is main source), estrogen (estriol E3 from DHEA-S — indicator of fetoplacental function), relaxin (cervical softening). FSH is NOT produced by the placenta — it is an anterior pituitary hormone. Reference: Guyton — Placental Hormones.

questions.questionText: Capacitation of spermatozoa refers to:
questions.optionB: Functional maturation of sperm in the female reproductive tract enabling fertilization
questions.optionD: Sperm transport to the vas deferens
questions.explanation: Capacitation: biochemical changes that sperm undergo in the female reproductive tract (uterus/fallopian tube) over ~7 hours. Involves: removal of cholesterol from sperm membrane, protein phosphorylation, increased membrane fluidity and Ca2+ permeability → hyperactivated motility and ability to undergo acrosome reaction. Acrosome reaction: release of enzymes (hyaluronidase, acrosin) to penetrate the zona pellucida. Capacitation occurs naturally or in vitro (for IVF). Reference: Guyton — Capacitation.

questions.questionText: The stages of puberty in females are described by:
questions.optionB: Tanner staging (breast and pubic hair development)
questions.explanation: Tanner stages (I-V): describe pubertal development of secondary sexual characteristics. Females: breast development + pubic hair. Males: genital development + pubic hair. Stage I: prepubertal. Stage V: adult. Female puberty order: thelarche (breast buds, first sign ~10-11 yrs) → pubarche/adrenarche → growth spurt → menarche (last event, ~12-13 yrs). First sign of puberty in males: testicular enlargement (>4 mL/2.5 cm). Reference: Guyton — Puberty.

questions.questionText: Prolactin promotes:
questions.optionB: Milk production (lactogenesis) and suppresses GnRH → lactational amenorrhea
questions.explanation: Prolactin functions: (1) Milk production (stimulates alveolar epithelial cells of the breast — lactogenesis), (2) Suppresses GnRH pulsatility → reduced LH/FSH → anovulation → lactational amenorrhea (natural contraception). Suckling is the strongest stimulus for prolactin release. Estrogen and progesterone during pregnancy stimulate breast development but block milk production. After delivery, drop in estrogen/progesterone → milk production begins. Reference: Guyton — Prolactin Lactation.

questions.questionText: The acrosome reaction involves:
questions.optionB: Release of enzymes from the sperm acrosome to penetrate the zona pellucida
questions.explanation: Acrosome reaction: after capacitation, when sperm contacts the zona pellucida (ZP3 glycoprotein triggers it), the acrosomal membrane fuses with sperm membrane → releases hydrolytic enzymes (hyaluronidase, acrosin, neuraminidase) that digest the zona pellucida, allowing sperm to reach the oocyte membrane. Only capacitated sperm can undergo acrosome reaction. After one sperm penetrates → cortical reaction → zona hardening → blocks polyspermy. Reference: Guyton — Fertilization.

questions.questionText: Spermatogenesis takes approximately:
questions.optionB: 64-74 days
questions.explanation: Spermatogenesis (spermatogonium → mature spermatozoa) takes ~64-74 days. Occurs in seminiferous tubules: spermatogonia (2n) → primary spermatocyte (2n) → meiosis I → secondary spermatocyte (n) → meiosis II → spermatid (n) → spermiogenesis → mature spermatozoa. Requires testosterone (from Leydig cells) and FSH (acts on Sertoli cells). Optimal temperature: 2-3°C below body temperature (hence testes in scrotum). Sperm maturation occurs in the epididymis (~12 days). Reference: Guyton — Spermatogenesis.

questions.questionText: Estrogen during the follicular phase causes:
questions.optionB: Endometrial proliferation (proliferative phase), thin watery cervical mucus (spinnbarkeit)
questions.explanation: Estrogen (from developing follicle, mainly estradiol E2): causes endometrial proliferation (gland growth, vascular development — proliferative phase), thins cervical mucus (watery, stretchable/spinnbarkeit — facilitates sperm entry), increases motility of fallopian tubes, and positive feedback on LH at high levels → LH surge. After ovulation, progesterone converts to secretory endometrium, thickens mucus. Reference: Guyton — Estrogen Effects.

questions.questionText: The blood-testis barrier is formed by:
questions.optionB: Tight junctions between adjacent Sertoli cells
questions.explanation: Blood-testis barrier: formed by tight junctions (occluding junctions) between adjacent Sertoli cells. Divides the seminiferous tubule into basal compartment (spermatogonia, accessible to blood) and adluminal compartment (spermatocytes, spermatids — immunologically privileged). Prevents immune cells from attacking developing sperm (which express novel antigens after meiosis). Disruption → autoimmune orchitis and infertility. Reference: Guyton — Blood-Testis Barrier.

questions.questionText: Menstruation occurs due to:
questions.optionB: Withdrawal of progesterone and estrogen (corpus luteum degeneration)
questions.explanation: In absence of pregnancy, corpus luteum degenerates (luteolysis) after ~14 days → progesterone and estrogen levels drop → endometrial spiral arteries constrict → ischemia → necrosis → shedding (menstruation, days 1-5). Prostaglandins (PGF2α) contribute to vasoconstriction and myometrial contractions (dysmenorrhea). NSAIDs reduce prostaglandins → relieve menstrual cramps. First day of menstruation = day 1 of the cycle. Reference: Guyton — Menstruation.

questions.questionText: The most reliable indicator of ovulation is:
questions.optionB: Rise in basal body temperature (progesterone effect) and LH surge
questions.explanation: Ovulation indicators: (1) LH surge in urine (ovulation predictor kits — most reliable for predicting imminent ovulation), (2) Basal body temperature rise of ~0.5°C (confirms ovulation has occurred — progesterone thermogenic effect), (3) Day 21 serum progesterone >3 ng/mL (confirms ovulation in 28-day cycle), (4) Cervical mucus changes (thin, stretchy = fertile). Ultrasound follicle tracking is the most accurate clinical method. Reference: Guyton — Ovulation Detection.

questions.questionText: 5-alpha reductase converts testosterone to:
questions.optionB: DHT (dihydrotestosterone)
questions.explanation: 5α-reductase converts testosterone → DHT (more potent androgen, 2-3x more active than testosterone). DHT is essential for: male external genitalia development, prostate growth, male-pattern hair (beard, body hair), male-pattern baldness, and sebaceous gland activity (acne). 5α-reductase deficiency: ambiguous genitalia at birth (female phenotype) → virilization at puberty ('guevedoces'). Finasteride (5α-reductase inhibitor): treats BPH and male-pattern baldness. Reference: Guyton — DHT.

questions.questionText: Implantation of the blastocyst occurs approximately on day:
questions.optionB: Day 6-7 after fertilization (day 20-21 of menstrual cycle)
questions.explanation: Implantation: blastocyst implants into the endometrium ~6-7 days after fertilization (day 20-21 of cycle). The trophoblast differentiates into cytotrophoblast and syncytiotrophoblast which invades the endometrium. Window of implantation: endometrium is receptive only during a narrow period (progesterone-dependent — pinopodes on endometrial surface). Most common site: upper posterior wall of uterus. Ectopic implantation most common in ampulla of fallopian tube. Reference: Guyton — Implantation.

questions.questionText: Aromatase enzyme converts:
questions.optionB: Androgens to estrogens
questions.explanation: Aromatase (CYP19A1): converts androgens to estrogens — testosterone → estradiol (E2), androstenedione → estrone (E1). Present in: ovarian granulosa cells (main source in premenopausal women), adipose tissue (main source in postmenopausal women — hence obesity → increased estrogen → increased breast cancer risk), placenta, brain, breast tissue. Aromatase inhibitors (letrozole, anastrozole): used in ER+ breast cancer in postmenopausal women. Reference: Guyton — Aromatase.

questions.questionText: FSH acts on which cells in the male reproductive system?
questions.optionB: Sertoli cells (supporting spermatogenesis)
questions.explanation: FSH acts on Sertoli cells in males: stimulates ABP (androgen-binding protein) production → concentrates testosterone in seminiferous tubules → supports spermatogenesis. Also stimulates inhibin B secretion (negative feedback on FSH). LH acts on Leydig cells → testosterone production. In females: FSH stimulates granulosa cell proliferation and follicular development; LH acts on theca cells (androgen production) and triggers ovulation. Reference: Guyton — FSH Action.

questions.questionText: The 'two-cell, two-gonadotropin' theory of ovarian steroidogenesis states that:
questions.optionB: Theca cells (LH → androgens) and granulosa cells (FSH → aromatase → estrogens) cooperate
questions.optionD: FSH and LH act on the same cell type
questions.explanation: Two-cell, two-gonadotropin model: LH acts on theca cells → produce androgens (androstenedione). Androgens diffuse to granulosa cells. FSH acts on granulosa cells → stimulates aromatase → converts androgens to estrogens (estradiol). Neither cell alone can produce significant estrogen. This model explains why both FSH and LH are needed for normal estrogen production and follicular development. Reference: Guyton — Ovarian Steroidogenesis.

questions.questionText: Relaxin is produced during pregnancy by the:
questions.optionB: Corpus luteum and placenta
questions.explanation: Relaxin: produced by the corpus luteum (main source in first trimester), decidua, and placenta. Functions: cervical softening and ripening, relaxation of pubic symphysis, inhibits uterine contractions (maintains pregnancy), remodeling of pelvic connective tissue for delivery, vasodilation (contributes to increased renal blood flow and GFR in pregnancy). Also has roles in males (from prostate — may affect sperm motility). Reference: Guyton — Relaxin.

questions.questionText: Semen analysis: normal sperm count is:
questions.optionB: ≥15 million/mL (WHO 2010 criteria)
questions.explanation: Normal semen parameters (WHO 2010): volume ≥1.5 mL, sperm concentration ≥15 million/mL, total count ≥39 million/ejaculate, motility ≥40% (progressive ≥32%), morphology ≥4% normal forms (strict Kruger criteria), pH 7.2-8.0. Oligozoospermia: <15 million/mL. Azoospermia: no sperm. Asthenozoospermia: poor motility. Teratozoospermia: abnormal morphology. Abstinence of 2-7 days before collection. Reference: Guyton — Semen Analysis.

questions.questionText: The cervical os is maximally dilated and mucus is thin and stretchable (spinnbarkeit) at the time of:
questions.optionB: Ovulation (peak estrogen effect)
questions.explanation: At ovulation (peak estrogen): cervical mucus becomes thin, watery, abundant, clear, and elastic/stretchable (spinnbarkeit) → sperm-friendly, facilitates passage. Ferning pattern on microscopy due to NaCl crystallization. During the luteal phase (progesterone dominance): mucus becomes thick, scant, viscous, hostile to sperm (cervical plug). This property is exploited by progestin-only contraceptives (thicken mucus → barrier to sperm). Reference: Guyton — Cervical Mucus.

questions.questionText: Complete meiosis in the female oocyte is completed at:
questions.optionB: Puberty
questions.optionC: After fertilization (meiosis II completed upon sperm entry)
questions.explanation: Female meiosis: Primary oocytes arrested at prophase I (diplotene stage) from fetal life. At puberty, LH surge → meiosis I completed just before ovulation → secondary oocyte + first polar body. Secondary oocyte begins meiosis II but arrests at metaphase II. Meiosis II is completed ONLY if fertilization occurs (sperm entry triggers cortical reaction and completion of meiosis II → ovum + second polar body). Unfertilized oocytes degenerate at metaphase II. Reference: Guyton — Oogenesis.
